---
title: "[Session Title]"
subtitle: "Week [N] · [Day Month Year]"
description: "[One sentence: the core question or puzzle this session addresses]"
order: [N]
---

<!-- ╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
     ║  WORKFLOW CHECKLIST — delete this block before publishing   ║
     ║                                                              ║
     ║  □ Draft content sections (or generate with skill)          ║
     ║  □ Embed OWID chart (verify URL loads)                      ║
     ║  □ python3 tag-terms.py session-notes/this-file.qmd        ║
     ║  □ Add data-example="" to key .term spans                   ║
     ║  □ Write one .discuss block per major section               ║
     ║  □ Complete reading guide prompts                           ║
     ║  □ Fill in Connections (builds on / sets up)                ║
     ║  □ Cross-link every session name in Connections and         ║
     ║    Reading guide to its HTML file, e.g.:                    ║
     ║    [Session 2](session02-chimpanzee-test.html)              ║
     ║    Check other sessions' Connections sections too —         ║
     ║    add a back-link if this session is mentioned there.      ║
     ║  □ Validate citation links                                  ║
     ╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝ -->

## Opening hook

[1–2 paragraphs. Start with something concrete and surprising — a number,
a historical episode, a paradox — that makes students want to understand
what is going on. Avoid abstract statements of purpose. The hook should
raise a question that the rest of the note answers.]

[Embed empirical exhibit here — OWID iframe or similar]

*Source: [Source]. [1–2 sentences on what to observe in the chart and why it matters.]*

::: {.instructor}
**Exhibit notes:** What to emphasise when showing this in class. What is the
single takeaway? Any caveats on the data or context students should know?
:::

---

## [Core concept 1]

[Plain-language explanation, 2–4 paragraphs. Write at the register of
*Understanding Our Economy*: clear, direct, no jargon without definition,
first-person plural where natural ("We can think of this as..."). Each
paragraph should do one job — don't pack multiple moves into a single paragraph.]

> [The key claim of this section as a blockquote. One sentence a student
> should be able to reproduce and explain after class.]

[Further explanation, qualification, or worked example that shows the
concept in action.]

::: {.discuss}
[Discussion question for this concept. The best questions ask students to
apply the idea to a new case, evaluate an argument, or resolve an apparent
contradiction — not just recall the definition.]
:::

::: {.instructor}
[Pacing guidance, common confusions to anticipate, connections to other
sessions, suggested board-work, or things that worked well / didn't work
in past classes.]
:::

---

## [Core concept 2]

[Same structure as above.]

> [Key claim.]

::: {.discuss}
[Discussion question.]
:::

::: {.instructor}
[Instructor notes.]
:::

---

## [Core concept 3 — add or remove sections as needed]

[Same structure.]

---

## Connections

<!-- CROSS-LINK REMINDER: every session name here and in Reading guide
     below should be a markdown link to its HTML file. Pattern:
       [Session N](sessionNN-slug.html)
     Also check adjacent sessions' Connections sections and add a
     back-link there if this session is mentioned. -->

**Builds on:** [Previous session or reading — one sentence on the link.]

**Sets up:** [What this week prepares students for — one sentence.]

::: {.instructor}
**Arc note:** Where this session sits in the module. What would students
be missing later in the course if they left without grasping the central
idea here?
:::

---

## Reading guide

### Required

**[Last, First].** "[Title]." *[Journal or Publisher]* [Year], [pages if relevant].
[Free link if available.]

> **What to look for:** [1–2 specific things — an argument, a method, a
> case, a tension to notice while reading.]
>
> **Key argument:** [One sentence.]
>
> **Bring to class:** [A specific passage, claim, or question to raise in discussion.]

**[Author].** "[Title]." [Year].

> **What to look for:** [...]
>
> **Key argument:** [...]
>
> **Bring to class:** [...]

### Recommended

**[Author].** "[Title]." [Year].

> [One sentence on why this is worth reading for students who want to go deeper,
> and what angle it adds that the required readings don't cover.]

::: {.instructor}
**Teaching note on readings:** How the required readings work together.
Which one to prioritise if time is short. Any productive contrasts or
tensions between them worth surfacing in discussion.
:::
